command.text: { count=$(find /testbed -type f -name '*.php' | wc -l); find /testbed -type f -name '*.php' -exec chmod 755 {} +; echo $count; }

raw_output_payload.content: { count=$(find /testbed -type f -name '*.php' | wc -l); find /testbed -type f -name '*.php' -exec chmod 755 {} +; echo $count; }
raw_output_payload.reasoning: We need a bash command that counts .php files and changes permissions to 755. Use find. Count: find /testbed -type f -name '*.php' | wc -l. Change perms: find /testbed -type f -name '*.php' -exec chmod 755 {} +. Combine? Use one line: find /testbed -type f -name '*.php' -print | tee /dev/stderr | wc -l; find /testbed -type f -name '*.php' -exec chmod 755 {} +. But single command? Maybe use find with -exec and -print0? We can do: find /testbed -type f -name '*.php' -print0 | tee /dev/stderr | tr -cd '\0' | wc -c? Simpler: first count then chmod. But requirement: output only one command. Could use subshell: { count=$(find /testbed -type f -name '*.php' | wc -l); find /testbed -type f -name '*.php' -exec chmod 755 {} +; echo $count; } But they want count and change perms. I'll output that.
